attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman, who is 36 weeks pregnant, presents to the Emergency Department with persistent dizziness, nausea and vomiting for the past two days. She says a feeling of dizziness started very suddenly two days ago right before she was going to bed, and she felt as though the room was spinning around her. Movement exacerbates her feeling of nausea and triggers vomiting. She states that she has not experienced feeling like this before. She reports no ringing in the ears, no sick contacts, no recent illness and no abdominal pain. She is usually well, and takes no medication other than pregnancy supplements.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following set of examination findings, if present, would suggest a peripheral cause of vertigo rather than a central cause?
options: Negative head impulse, bidirectional nystagmus, vertical skew present
Negative head impulse, vertical nystagmus, vertical skew present
Negative head impulse, unidirectional nystagmus, no vertical skew
Positive head impulse, unidirectional nystagmus, no vertical skew
Positive head impulse, bidirectional nystagmus, vertical skew present
attributes.explanation:
This patient is experiencing vertigo, defined as a hallucination of movement (often described as a sensation of the body or surroundings spinning) caused by vestibular dysfunction.

Peripheral causes of vertigo are often localised to the inner ear and include BPPV, vestibular neuronitis, labrynthitis, and Meniere’s disease. Central causes of vertigo often indicate a problem with the brain, such as a cerebellar stroke, space-occupying lesion, migraine or multiple sclerosis.

The HINTS examination (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) is used in order to differentiate between peripheral vs central causes of vertigo.

Head impulse - this is also referred to as the horizontal head impulse test. The purpose of this test is to test for the function of the vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR), and hence the vestibulocochlear nerve.

This entails having the patient fixed their gaze on something nearby, such as the nose of the examiner. The examiner will place their hands on the lateral sides of the head, and then rotate the head horizontally. If there are secondary eye movements that occur to bring the eyes back on the target (known as corrective saccades), this indicates that there is a positive test - which indicates a peripheral cause of vertigo due to an issues with the vestibulococchlear nerve . A lack of corrective saccades indicates that there is not not an issue with the VOR, and could occur for a central cause of vertigo (this would be a negative head impulse test).

Nystagmus - involves tracking the gaze of the patient from left to right without fixating on an object. The nystagmus is described by the direction of the beating of the pupil (direction of the saccadic eye movement).

Unidirectional nystagmus, horizontal nystagmus, or no nystagmus, are more likely to be peripheral in origin. Bidirectional nystagmus or vertical nystagmus are more likely to be central in origin. Bidirectional nystagmus is highly specific for stroke.

Test of Skew - this test for vertical ocular malignment (which is a positive test of skew / sign of vertical skew), and occurs in a central cause of vertigo . The test of skew involves having the patient focus their gaze on the examiner's nose when they stand in front of them. The examiner will then cover a patient's eye with a hand, and then quickly move the hand to cover the other eye. If there is any vertical misalignment (vertical skew) that can be seen with the test, the result is positive and indicates a central cause of vertigo.

Putting together the findings of these tests, only one of the following needs to be present for a patient with vertigo to be considered as a concerning finding for a central cause: negative head impulse test result, nystagmus that is either vertical or bidirectional, positive test of skew (vertical skew).

If all of the following are present, it is reassuring and indicative of a peripheral cause: positive head impulse test result, nystagmus that is unidirectional, and a negative test of skew (absence of vertical skew).

Therefore, a HINTS examination finding of a positive head impulse, unidirectional nystagmus, and no vertical skew is correct, as it is the only option demonstrating reassuring findings which are most indicative of a peripheral cause of vertigo.
learningPoints: The HINTS exam is used to differentiate between peripheral and central causes of vertigo

Reassuring findings for the HINTS exam in a patient with vertigo are when all of the following are occurring: positive head impulse test result, nystagmus that is unidirectional, and a negative test of skew (no vertical skew).
references: The HINTS Exam to Differentiate Central from Peripheral Vertigo - Emergency Medicine. Emergency Medicine. 2014. Available from: https://emergencymedicine.wustl.edu/items/the-hints-exam-to-differentiate-central-from-peripheral-vertigo/
Herr RD, Zun L, Mathews JJ. A directed approach to the dizzy patient. Ann Emerg Med. 1989 Jun;18(6):664-72. doi: 10.1016/s0196-0644(89)80524-4. PMID: 2729692.

Kattah, J. C., Talkad, A. V., Wang, D. Z., Hsieh, Y. H., & Newman-Toker, D. E. (2009). HINTS to diagnose stroke in the acute vestibular syndrome: three-step bedside oculomotor examination more sensitive than early MRI diffusion-weighted imaging. Stroke, 40(11), 3504-3510.

Iwasaki S, Kamogashira T, Fujimoto C, Kabaya K, Kinoshita M and Yamasoba T (2022) The Role of Neck Input in Producing Corrective Saccades in the Head Impulse Test. Front. Neurol. 13:881411. doi: 10.3389/fneur.2022.881411
Cockerham, Olmos (2008). Chapter 26 - Orbital and Ocular Manifestations of Ocular Disease. In Neurology and General Medicine (Fourth Edition). 483-501. Book.
